reasoning: The spread was 0.00 as of August 15. To have "increased", it must be > 0.00 on the resolution dates. For the 1-week horizon (Aug 24), the FRED statistical trend model estimates a 26% chance of the spread exceeding 0.00, reflecting slight downward momentum in recent days after a temporary peak. For the Sept 16 horizon, the value is just before the heavily anticipated Sept 17 FOMC rate cut; at this point, the EFFR will still be elevated (4.33), but the 10-year yield (DGS10) will likely price in the rate cut and remain lower, keeping the spread compressed or negative. Hence, the probability remains low. By Nov 15, the Fed is expected to have executed one or two rate cuts, bringing the EFFR down. This structural shift typically leads to a yield curve steepening, making a positive spread much more likely. However, for the longest forecasting horizons (Nov 2025 and Feb 2026), systemic macroeconomic uncertainty increases significantly. Following standard superforecasting principles and the explicit system prompt instructions, uncertainty is scaled up with the forecast horizon, bringing probabilities for the distant dates progressively closer to 0.5.
